**Talking Pictures TV** (**TPTV**) is a British [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air") vintage film and nostalgia television channel. It was launched on 26 May 2015 on [Sky](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK").[[Note 1]](#cite_note-6)[[2]](#cite_note-Express-2) Later it also became available on [Freeview](/wiki/Freeview_(UK) "Freeview (UK)"), [Freesat](/wiki/Freesat "Freesat") and [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media "Virgin Media").[[1]](#cite_note-FAQs-1)[[6]](#cite_note-7)

It is on air 24 hours a day and features mainly older British films, both classics and [B-films](/wiki/B-movie "B-movie").[[7]](#cite_note-Barnett-8) The schedule also includes some American films, straight-to-video programmes, cinema shorts, extended interviews with veteran actors and period [home movies](/wiki/Home_movies "Home movies") of British locations.

In July 2018, Talking Pictures TV had an audience share of 0.50%. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit), it had reached an audience share of 0.63%.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Foundation")]

TPTV is a [family business](/wiki/Family_business "Family business"), founded by producer/editor Noel Cronin, and run by his daughter Sarah Cronin-Stanley with her husband Neill Stanley.[[7]](#cite_note-Barnett-8) Films in many genres ([horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film"), [comedy](/wiki/Comedy_film "Comedy film"), [drama](/wiki/Drama_film "Drama film") and [thriller](/wiki/Thriller_(genre) "Thriller (genre)")) are included in the schedule. In-house productions are also shown, as are items from the Cronins' own back catalogue,[[9]](#cite_note-Watford_Observer-10) including Noel's Dandelion Distribution, as well as series from the archive of [Southern Television](/wiki/Southern_Television "Southern Television"), a [former ITV contractor](/wiki/History_of_ITV "History of ITV"), and early American television shows. Movies are usually copied directly from [film reels](/wiki/Film_reel "Film reel"); damaged reels from the catalogue are often replaced by donations, either from online, viewers or available libraries.[[10]](#cite_note-SCS_podcast-11)

One of the aims of the founders was to maintain the history of British cinema; it was said to have been in the making since 2007.[[11]](#cite_note-12) Older movies, including those made in black and white, were once regularly shown on terrestrial channels, such as BBC and [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"),[[10]](#cite_note-SCS_podcast-11) but such programming had declined by the 2000s, with only the best known classic films occasionally being aired.

Cronin-Stanley explained to the *Watford Observer* in 2016 that "People were interested in the big titles but he [her father Noel] wanted to save the smaller, more obscure titles, from getting [lost](/wiki/Missing_film "Missing film")".[[9]](#cite_note-Watford_Observer-10) However, television networks rejected their [pitches](/wiki/Sales_pitch "Sales pitch"), unconvinced that it would be of high demand,[[10]](#cite_note-SCS_podcast-11) so they decided to set up the channel [independently](/wiki/Independent_station "Independent station").[[9]](#cite_note-Watford_Observer-10) Cronin-Stanley later explained that the channel specialises in "the things people have forgotten".[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Availability")]

Talking Pictures TV was initially only available via digital satellite on the Sky platform in the United Kingdom and Ireland.[[2]](#cite_note-Express-2) Since 15 September 2015, the channel has also been available on Freeview. It became available on Freesat channel 306 in February 2016,[[1]](#cite_note-FAQs-1) and on [Virgin Media](/wiki/Virgin_Media "Virgin Media") on 1 June 2017. On Freeview, it was initially broadcast only in [standard definition](/wiki/Standard-definition_television "Standard-definition television") (SDTV) on a HD multiplex and could be received only by Freeview HD equipment.[[1]](#cite_note-FAQs-1)

From 30 November 2017, the channel became available, to those within coverage (90 percent of UK households), on any TV capable of receiving Freeview.[[13]](#cite_note-14) In 2018, the channel reportedly had a weekly audience of about 2 million,[[14]](#cite_note-Moore-15) but [BBC News](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News") reported that it possibly rose to as high as 6 million in mid-2020 (during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic")).[[15]](#cite_note-16)

### Television series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Television series")]

Talking Pictures TV features several classic television series from American broadcasters [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), and [NBC](/wiki/National_Broadcasting_Company "National Broadcasting Company"), as well as British series, mostly from [Southern Television](/wiki/Southern_Television "Southern Television") productions for the [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_channel) "ITV (TV channel)") network. Many of the American television shows were produced by [Four Star Television](/wiki/Four_Star_Television "Four Star Television").

New television programmes for 2022 included *The Heritage Chart Show with Mike Read*, a pop music countdown of videos and live performances from veteran acts, presented on Sunday nights by the former Radio 1 disc jockey [Mike Read](/wiki/Mike_Read "Mike Read") and shared with the [Local TV](/wiki/Local_Television_Limited "Local Television Limited") network of channels.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) Also Read can be seen each week with Talking Pictures TV founder Noel Cronin on the channel's archive programme *The Footage Detectives*, a show which discusses forgotten films and lost TV shows such as *The Barnstormers* from 1964.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other")]

Talking Pictures TV also features several films from the series featuring [Old Mother Riley](/wiki/Old_Mother_Riley "Old Mother Riley"), and from the [British Film Institute](/wiki/British_Film_Institute "British Film Institute")'s (BFI) archives, including several significant documentaries, and a considerable number of [Children's Film Foundation](/wiki/Children%27s_Film_Foundation "Children's Film Foundation") productions, named under a series segment *Glimpses*.[[7]](#cite_note-Barnett-8) Its intention is for both nostalgia and education, as well as reminding the audience of the past; some footage is donated by viewers.[[10]](#cite_note-SCS_podcast-11) The series is the most popular original content on the channel and its compilation DVD is the most popular buy on Renown Film's website.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The *Take Two* series was presented by Elstree historian Morris Bright, and occasionally Robert Ross and/or the channel creators, who interview famous actors whose films and programming is airing on the channel, such as [Nanette Newman](/wiki/Nanette_Newman "Nanette Newman"), [Angela Douglas](/wiki/Angela_Douglas "Angela Douglas"), Rita Tushingham,[[128]](#cite_note-129) [Sylvia Syms](/wiki/Sylvia_Syms "Sylvia Syms"), [Michael Craig](/wiki/Michael_Craig_(actor) "Michael Craig (actor)"),[[129]](#cite_note-130) [Shirley Eaton](/wiki/Shirley_Eaton "Shirley Eaton"), [Sally Thomsett](/wiki/Sally_Thomsett "Sally Thomsett"), [Jack Smethurst](/wiki/Jack_Smethurst "Jack Smethurst"), [Jenny Hanley](/wiki/Jenny_Hanley "Jenny Hanley"), [Norman Eshley](/wiki/Norman_Eshley "Norman Eshley"), [Valerie Leon](/wiki/Valerie_Leon "Valerie Leon"), [Madeline Smith](/wiki/Madeline_Smith "Madeline Smith"), [Marc Sinden](/wiki/Marc_Sinden "Marc Sinden"), and [Janette Scott](/wiki/Janette_Scott "Janette Scott");[[130]](#cite_note-WH_Times-131) a few of them recorded [station idents](/wiki/Station_identification "Station identification") for the channel.[[131]](#cite_note-Douglas_ident-132)[[132]](#cite_note-Sinden_ident-133)[[133]](#cite_note-Smethurst_ident-134)[[134]](#cite_note-Eshley_ident-135)[[135]](#cite_note-Syms_ident-136)[[136]](#cite_note-Thomsett_ident-137)[[137]](#cite_note-Leon_ident-138)[[138]](#cite_note-Smith_ident-139) An interview with *[A Family at War](/wiki/A_Family_at_War "A Family at War")* creator John Finch was also aired,[[139]](#cite_note-Finch_interview-140) as well as *Neil Sean Meets...*, an interviewing series presented by [Neil Sean](/wiki/Neil_Sean "Neil Sean"), who has interviewed historians and celebrities of the mid-20th century, such as [Tommy Steele](/wiki/Tommy_Steele "Tommy Steele"),[[140]](#cite_note-141) [Roger Moore](/wiki/Roger_Moore "Roger Moore")[[141]](#cite_note-142) and [Eden Kane](/wiki/Eden_Kane "Eden Kane").

Interviews from 2018 onwards have been filmed in the [Cinema Museum](/wiki/Cinema_Museum_(London) "Cinema Museum (London)") in the wake of the museum's [foreclosure](/wiki/Foreclosure "Foreclosure") announcement;[[142]](#cite_note-143) the interviewees there include [Mark Lester](/wiki/Mark_Lester "Mark Lester"), [George Layton](/wiki/George_Layton "George Layton"),[[143]](#cite_note-144) [Anita Harris](/wiki/Anita_Harris "Anita Harris"), [Sid James](/wiki/Sid_James "Sid James")'s daughter [Reina](/wiki/Reina_James "Reina James"),[[144]](#cite_note-145) [Anneke Wills](/wiki/Anneke_Wills "Anneke Wills"),[[145]](#cite_note-Wills-146) and [Peter Butterworth](/wiki/Peter_Butterworth "Peter Butterworth")'s son Tyler.[[146]](#cite_note-147) Some interviews in the series were later released on a DVD collection named *Talking Pictures with...*.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

### Controversy, racial slurs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Controversy, racial slurs")]

The regulator [Ofcom](/wiki/Ofcom "Ofcom") has warned the channel about the use of [racial slurs](/wiki/Racial_slur "Racial slur") in its programming on a handful of occasions,[[14]](#cite_note-Moore-15) including an episode of [Granada Television](/wiki/ITV_Granada "ITV Granada")'s *[A Family at War](/wiki/A_Family_at_War "A Family at War")*, originally broadcast in the early 1970s, shown by TPTV in the hour just before the 9pm [watershed](/wiki/Watershed_(broadcasting) "Watershed (broadcasting)"), and an uncensored interview with [Joan Turner](/wiki/Joan_Turner "Joan Turner") during her appearance on the talk show *Tell Me Another*.[[104]](#cite_note-TMA-105)

Cronin-Stanley told *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* in February 2018: "Ofcom say we need to advise people before they watch something that it may contain outdated racial stereotyping, but I would say that's babysitting our audience".[[14]](#cite_note-Moore-15) However, since late 2018, warning notices have been displayed just before some programmes begin, stating, for example, that a programme "was made between 1978 and 1992" and that "Some viewers may be offended by the language and attitudes expressed by some characters ... which reflect the time it was made."[[Note 2]](#cite_note-178) In addition, occurrences of such terms may be removed from the soundtrack and the subtitles.[[Note 3]](#cite_note-179)

## Related projects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Related projects")]

### Renown Pictures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Renown Pictures")]

For over 20 years, Cronin-Stanley and Cronin have been patrons of a DVD membership club, The Renown Film Club, for fans of B-film classics.[[2]](#cite_note-Express-2) Cronin had worked for [The Rank Organisation](/wiki/The_Rank_Organisation "The Rank Organisation") and [Central Office of Information](/wiki/Central_Office_of_Information "Central Office of Information"), and bought the rights to several libraries that owned films that air on TPTV,[[9]](#cite_note-Watford_Observer-10) such as much of the Southern Television library, and originally licensed them out to [terrestrial channels](/wiki/Terrestrial_channel "Terrestrial channel").[[7]](#cite_note-Barnett-8) Many of the films aired on Talking Pictures TV are available to buy on DVD, often sold as compilations under different genres, through the distributing company,[[177]](#cite_note-Renown-180) both online and through the Renown's telephone service. Each DVD set is full of films made between the 1930s and 1960s, and also include special features, such as interviews and history. Other merchandise is also sold on the website, such as mugs, clothing, biographies, and calendars (available to buy in December for the new year), often branded under Talking Pictures TV's name. In spring 2019, Renown released its first CD compilation album named *Hits From the Flicks*, three CDs of songs from musicals and movies with pop song theme tunes that had aired on the channel.[[178]](#cite_note-181)

### The Renown Film Festival

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Renown Film Festival")]

Since 2014, the channel's producers created the Renown Film Festival, which celebrates classic British film, featuring guest appearances by actors whose films have been aired, as well as historians, who give interviews; there are also screenings for films that were once considered [lost](/wiki/Missing_film "Missing film"), and memorabilia available to buy and be autographed by the actors. It occurs annually in February, and tickets are advertised on both TPTV's website and the channel. Actors who have appeared on the guest lists include [Jess Conrad](/wiki/Jess_Conrad "Jess Conrad") (who reunited with the remaining cast of *[The Boys](/wiki/The_Boys_(1962_British_film) "The Boys (1962 British film)")* in 2017),[[179]](#cite_note-182) [Rita Tushingham](/wiki/Rita_Tushingham "Rita Tushingham"),[[180]](#cite_note-Renown_Festival_2018-183) [Brian Murphy](/wiki/Brian_Murphy_(actor) "Brian Murphy (actor)"), [Melvyn Hayes](/wiki/Melvyn_Hayes "Melvyn Hayes"), and [Derren Nesbitt](/wiki/Derren_Nesbitt "Derren Nesbitt").[[180]](#cite_note-Renown_Festival_2018-183)

The festival takes place in [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), such as Watersmeet Theatre, [Rickmansworth](/wiki/Rickmansworth "Rickmansworth")[[180]](#cite_note-Renown_Festival_2018-183) and [St. Albans](/wiki/St._Albans "St. Albans")' The Alban Arena,[[181]](#cite_note-184) but 2019 was the first year to have more than one festival in a year; October 2019 was the Renown Film's 6th Festival of Film at the [Stockport Plaza](/wiki/Stockport_Plaza "Stockport Plaza"),[[182]](#cite_note-185) which also featured special guests, film screenings, and memorabilia. Although tickets sold out, St. Albans' 2020 Festival was postponed due to the [coronavirus outbreak](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[183]](#cite_note-186)

### Podcast

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Podcast")]

A spin-off [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") named *The Talking Pictures TV Podcast* was announced in December 2018[[184]](#cite_note-podcast-187) by Adam Roche,[[185]](#cite_note-Waterman-188) the creator of the podcasts *The Secret History of Hollywood* and *Attaboy Clarence*,[[185]](#cite_note-Waterman-188) and began broadcasting on 3 February 2019. Each episode is uploaded monthly to coincide with TPTV's schedule releases and invites the audience to review movies and television programmes that will broadcast on the channel during that month. Its first episode became the most popular podcast in the UK [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") chart within roughly five hours.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

### TPTV Encore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Talking_Pictures_TV&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: TPTV Encore")]

On 1 December 2021, TPTV Encore launched.[[187]](#cite_note-190)[[188]](#cite_note-191) It is Talking Pictures TV's official [streaming service](/wiki/Streaming_service "Streaming service") for viewers to catch up with the latest media recently aired on the channel, as well as hosting Renown Pictures' Southern TV archive.[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193)[[191]](#cite_note-194)
